turns.user_message: I have been smoking two packs a day for 20 years.
step2_rag_chunks: Avoid keeping cigarettes, lighters, or ashtrays nearby when trying to quit smoking.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

Patients who smoke their first cigarette within 30 minutes of waking up, or who smoke more than 15 cigarettes per day, often have strong physical nicotine dependence. For these patients, combining behavioral strategies with nicotine replacement therapy gives better results than behavioral strategies alone.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the EXPLORATION phase. You know the basics. Now gently explore what situations or emotions make them smoke more — triggers like stress, meals, social settings, boredom. Do NOT suggest any strategies yet. Just understand their situation.

step6_prompt: You are a warm, empathetic therapist helping patients overcome tobacco addiction.
STYLE RULES — follow these strictly:
- Keep your response to 1-3 sentences maximum
- Ask only ONE question per response
- Do NOT list techniques, bullet points, or dump information
- First acknowledge what the patient said, then ask ONE follow-up question
- Only suggest a technique when you are in the Planning phase
- Use simple, conversational language — not clinical or formal
[Session Phase: 2 — Exploration]

turns.user_message: I smoke after every meal, with my morning coffee, and when I feel anxious.
step2_rag_chunks: Many people smoke out of habit during specific routines such as after meals, while drinking tea or coffee, or when talking with friends.
Stress is a common trigger for smoking. Relaxation techniques such as deep breathing, meditation, or short walks can help manage stress without using tobacco.
Morning cravings are common for people who smoke regularly. Drinking water, brushing your teeth, or going for a short walk can help reduce the urge to smoke.

step4_session_phase.phase_instruction: You are in the MOTIVATION phase. You understand their triggers. Now explore their motivation: why do they want to quit — is it health, family, cost, doctor advice? Have they tried before and what happened? Be empathetic. Do NOT push strategies yet.

turns.user_message: I want to quit because I had chest pain last month and my doctor is worried.
step2_rag_chunks: Quitting smoking reduces the risk of lung disease, heart disease, and many types of cancer.
Within weeks of quitting smoking, breathing and circulation often improve.
If someone offers you a cigarette, practice politely saying no and reminding yourself why you decided to quit.

turns.user_message: I tried nicotine gum, patches, and cold turkey. None of them worked for long.
step2_rag_chunks: Nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum, patches, or lozenges can help reduce withdrawal symptoms and cravings.
Replacing smoking with another activity such as chewing gum, eating healthy snacks, or walking can help break the habit.

When a patient has tried to quit multiple times and keeps relapsing, or reports strong physical cravings that are hard to manage, nicotine replacement therapy such as nicotine gum or patches is worth discussing as an additional support tool alongside behavioral strategies.
